1: The oracle concerning Moab: Ar and Kir, the two major cities, are destroyed in a single night. The speed — a single night — echoes the night of YHWH's judgments in Exodus (the angel of death at Passover) and the night fall of Babylon (Daniel 5:30). Moab was Israel's neighbor and traditional enemy (Genesis 19:37), yet also the homeland of Ruth — the Gentile ancestor of David. The ambiguity of Moab (enemy and ancestor of the messianic line) is part of the prophetic tension: even Moab's fall evokes sorrow (vv 5, Isaiah 16:11).
2: The people of Moab go up to their temples and high places to weep, with shaved heads and cut-off beards — the bodily markers of extreme mourning in the ancient world. Their grief is religious: they seek their gods in the crisis. In the NT, the contrast is the mourning that leads to comfort: blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted (Matthew 5:4) — but this is mourning for sin before YHWH, not religious mourning directed toward false gods. Moab's mourning is directed to the wrong address.
3: In the streets they wear sackcloth; on rooftops and squares everyone mourns and weeps uncontrollably. The public, comprehensive grief of Moab is genuine — Isaiah does not mock it but records it with sympathy (v 5: my heart cries out for Moab). The prophetic empathy for the nation under judgment anticipates the NT posture of mourning with those who mourn (Romans 12:15). YHWH's judgment is real and severe; the grief it produces is also real. The compassion of the prophet for the judged nation is a small image of the divine compassion that will ultimately extend even to Moab (Ruth's lineage reaching David and the Messiah).
4: Heshbon and Elealeh raise their cry — even Moab's soldiers shudder. The sound of lamentation spreads geographically through Moab's territory: city after city is engulfed. The soldiers' shuddering signals total military collapse — the army that might defend Moab is as demoralized as the civilian population. In the Christological reading, this comprehensive grief-spreading is the dark image of what judgment day looks like when it arrives before the kingdom is secured through the Servant's work.
5: My heart cries out for Moab. The speaker — Isaiah, and perhaps YHWH through him — mourns for the enemy nation. The divine mourning over judgment is a consistent thread in prophetic literature (Jeremiah 48:31-36 expands this lament for Moab; Ezekiel 33:11: I take no pleasure in the death of the wicked). Jesus expresses the same grief over Jerusalem: how often I wanted to gather your children together, but you were unwilling (Matthew 23:37). The God who judges is the same God who mourns the necessity of judgment — both are expressions of his holiness and love.
6: The pools of Nimrim have dried up — grass withered, new growth gone, nothing green. The drying up of water sources is the ecological sign of YHWH's withdrawal of blessing (cf. Amos 4:7-8). The greening of a land depended on its water sources; the drying up of Nimrim signals that the land itself is dying along with the civilization. In the Christological framework, the rivers of living water that the Messiah provides (John 7:38, Isaiah 44:3) are the positive reversal of this ecological death — the Servant brings the waters of life where judgment has dried up every spring.
7: Whatever they have accumulated and saved they carry across the Brook of the Willows. The refugees of Moab flee with their accumulated wealth across the border brook — carrying what they can before it is taken from them. The brook becomes the liminal boundary between the old life (destroyed Moab) and the uncertain future. In the NT, the possessions-carried-into-exile motif contrasts with the treasure stored in heaven that moth and rust cannot destroy (Matthew 6:19-20) — the wealth the refugee truly needs is the one that cannot be taken at the border.
8: The sound of weeping has encircled the whole border of Moab — the wailing reaches even Eglaim and Beer-elim. The lamentation is geographically comprehensive: no part of Moab escapes it. The encircling grief mirrors the encircling army — YHWH's judgment is as complete in its mourning as it is in its military form. In the NT, the weeping and gnashing of teeth (Matthew 8:12, Isaiah 22:13, Isaiah 25:30) that marks the outer darkness is the eschatological form of this comprehensive mourning: those outside the kingdom will mourn the loss of what they could have had.
9: For the waters of Dimon run red with blood; and I will bring more upon Dimon — a lion against the fugitives of Moab and against what remains in the land. The blood in the waters echoes the first plague of Egypt (Exodus 7:17-21) — YHWH's judgment on Egypt is now applied to Moab. The lion against the fugitives is the predator that hunts down those who flee the military disaster: there is no safe exit. In the broader Isaianic trajectory, the lion that destroys is answered by the lion and lamb lying down together (Isaiah 11:6, Isaiah 65:25) — the messianic peace transforms the predator. The eschatological reversal of this lion-against-fugitives is the vision of universal safety in the new creation.
